data.title: Devon Energy Has Continued Room To Grow
data.description:
Devon Energy (NYSE: DVN) is a hydrocarbon company worth almost $24 billion. The company has an incredibly focused U.S.-based portfolio, with double-digit cash flow production and a commitment to generating strong shareholder returns in a variety of markets. It's slightly outperformed the market since we last recommended investing but has continued growth room.

As we'll see, strong share price weakness since 2022 makes the company a valuable investment.

Devon Energy Q3 Highlights

Devon Energy had a strong Q3 with impressive performance across the board.

The company delivered 390k barrels/day, near top-end guidance. At the same time, the company managed to do that with a capital investment of $860 million, 5% below guidance. That's led to strong mid-single-digit improvement in operating cost. The company is ahead of schedule on cost optimizations, which stack with this increased efficiency.

Putting all this together, the company has been able to drive substantial shareholder returns. It's retired debt ahead of schedules and reduced its outstanding share count by 13% in just a few years. The company has continued to look at opportunistic add-ons to improve its asset portfolio and, for 2026, expects to achieve further efficiency.

Devon Energy had more than $2 billion in FCF in 3 quarters, putting it at a double-digit FCF yield. The scale of the companies optimization improvements is visible in the chart above. It's exceedingly important to see a company that can outperform in every segment and carry that down to a bottom line in terms of cash flow and shareholder returns.

Devon Energy Portfolio Improvements

Breaking down the company's portfolio improvements, Devon Energy has been focused on driving strong cash flow.

Devon Energy has consistently reduced its capital spending from its 2024 guidance by a stunning $425 million as efficiency has improved. Its preliminary 2026 guidance of $3.6 billion shows a further ability to improve that number further. At the same time, the company has walked up its production guidance by almost 3% to 388 thousand barrels/day.

That double-digit capital efficiency improvement means substantial improvement in FCF / barrel, perfect for a tough environment.

Devon Energy's initiatives and continued improvements are visible above. Capital efficiency has seen $225 million in improvements for 2025, and the company is continuing to chase additional opportunities. Across the board, the company has roughly $1 billion in opportunities to reduce its costs, which should help drive additional returns.

Devon Energy Financial Metrics

Looking at Devon Energy, the company is well positioned to drive additional shareholder returns.

In 3Q 2025, the company had $820 million in FCF, annualized at ~15%. The company managed to buy back shares at a 4% rate and pay a dividend equivalent to a 2.5% yield for shareholders. That's a high single-digit return. The company managed to do that while retiring $485 million of debt, or 2% of its market cap.

That enables the company to save on debt and provide additional shareholder returns.

Devon Energy has a 0.9x net debt-to-EBITDAX with $2.5 billion of absolute debt expected to be retired. With a $1.3 billion cash position, the company can comfortably afford that, and its recent payment is a great start in that direction. The company has a strong investment-grade credit rating. Clearing up the balance sheet will save on interest costs in a high-yield environment.

It'll also enable the company to focus on additional shareholder returns past what's already an impressive rate of return.

Devon Energy 2026 Outlook

Devon Energy has released a preliminary 2026 outlook that looks great.

The company's breakeven funding level, which includes its dividend, is at <$45/barrel. Total capital is expected to come in at $3.6 billion which includes production growth versus simply maintaining production. At $60 WTI, which is around current prices, the company has an impressive 13% FCF yield, and at $70 WTI, it grows to 20% FCF yield.

Devon Energy and Coterra

Another major potential development is rumors that Coterra Energy and Devon Energy are merging.

This could make for a $50 billion megacap oil company, a combined shale company. Coterra Energy has been under an activist push to revamp the company, but a potential merger here could enable substantial cost savings and shareholder returns. We cannot speculate further until seeing the merger agreement, but given Devon Energy's strong culture, we're optimistic of management's ability to pick an effective deal.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices and natural gas prices. Natural gas is doing quite well, benefiting from artificial intelligence datacenter demand; however, that's not guaranteed to continue. A downturn there could have a strong impact on natural gas prices. Similarly, trade wars, etc., could hurt oil prices, hurting shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Devon Energy has an impressive portfolio of assets. More so, they have a management team that doesn't overrun their bounds. Instead, Devon Energy focuses on modestly expanding production while focusing even more on reducing cost per barrel to improve FCF. At the same time, Devon Energy has impressively grown its FCF.

The company has focused on strong shareholder returns for years. It's maintained a very respectable 2.5% dividend, and it's continued to aggressively repurchase shares. A double-digit reduction in its float means stronger future returns. The Coterra merger remains an open question, but overall we see strong potential.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: NMAI: Diversified Portfolio At A Hefty Discount, But Mixed Results
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The last time we gave Nuveen Multi-Asset Income Fund (NMAI) a look, it was still quite a young fund, less than a year old. That said, it was the combination of a few funds that merged into this "new" closed-end fund. This CEF provides significant diversification through investing in a hybrid portfolio that includes equity and fixed-income securities. This provides significant diversification to the managers of this fund, being able to invest where and how much they want into whatever they feel is the best opportunity.

The fund slid to a substantially wide discount as we noted in our initial coverage. That discount has narrowed since some of the widest levels, but it remains trading at near double-digit discount levels. With that, it still has some appeal to consider for some investors, but this looks to be about what the "normal" discount level could be. That would generally mean it isn't looking like too much of a bargain today, but also not overly expensive.

NMAI Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.48
  • Discount/Premium: -9.09%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.63%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.44%
  • Leverage: 30.48%
  • Managed Assets: $688.2 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

NMAI's investment objective is "to provide attractive total return through high current income and capital appreciation."

To achieve this, the fund "dynamically invests in a portfolio of equity and debt securities of issuers located around the world. This dynamic investment strategy uses a risk-based framework in which any amount can be allocated to an asset class at any time. The Fund may invest in equity and debt securities of any type without limit. The relative allocations of the Fund's managed assets for investment between equity and debt securities, and relative allocations to the different types of equity and income strategies, will vary from time to time, consistent with the Fund's investment objective."

Recently, the fund managers have seen a shake-up; however, neither the investment policies nor the investment objective has been altered.

Effective 07 Jan 2026, Mark Zheng, James Kim and John Tribolet became portfolio managers of the Fund. Nathan Shetty and Anurag Dugar are no longer managers of the Fund. This update is not expected to impact the overall investment strategy.

The fund is leveraged, and that's through both borrowings and reverse repurchase agreements, both of which come with financing costs that are based on short-term borrowings. Therefore, as the Fed cuts its benchmark rate, the cost of financing has been gradually coming down. However, leverage always increases risks due to increased volatility and downside potential. It also comes with those expenses that increase the fund's overall total expense ratio to 2.98%.

Performance - Discount Narrows But Remains Rather Wide

Since our last write-up, the fund's total returns have been fairly solid but have underperformed the S&P 500 Index.

However, given the dynamic hybrid approach, the fund considers a blended benchmark that includes 50% MSCI All Country World Index, 35% Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Bond Index and 15% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. That helps to reflect that the fund is generally 50% positioned in common stocks and the remainder in fixed-income instruments, and provides a better overall barometer for benchmarking.

They provide the annualized performance comparison in annual and semi-annual reports, which can make them a bit dated. However, what we can see in the latest semi-annual report is that the fund has performed fairly respectably against its blended benchmark on a total NAV return basis in the last one-year period. On the other hand, the fund's performance since its inception on both the share price and the NAV was rather weak overall.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results, but these are not results to necessarily brag about anyway. At the same time, the latest results appear to be a bit more encouraging for the fund.

During this period, the fund's share price was negatively impacted relative to the NAV performance due to a sizeable discount opening up. That has since narrowed, but it still remains relatively wide compared to all CEF discount averages that come in at -5.2%. For hybrid/balanced funds, the average is a bit wider at -7.9%—and NMAI is currently wider than that.

So while the fund is trading at a relatively narrower discount compared to its own average, it could still be viewed as relatively attractive. In terms of rank performance against its peers that CEFData lists, we see that the fund has been fairly mixed. Coming in fairly strong over the last year, but then the 3-year period was a fairly weaker performer in terms of total NAV returns and total share price results.

Saba Capital Management holds a position in this fund, but they had been regularly selling out of the fund in 2024 and 2025. That's left them with a fairly minimal ownership percentage of less than 5% most recently. Therefore, that is one less pressure on the fund as of late.

Distribution - Looking Enticing, But Long-Term Sustainability Questioned

The fund initially launched with a quarterly distribution policy but more recently switched to a monthly payer. This tends to be more appealing to income investors and can generate more interest. However, the fund also reduced its distribution recently, too, which is a negative sign for most investors. For me, when funds pay high distribution rates and they adjust the payout from time to time, it doesn't necessarily bother me, but it can be a catalyst to keep a fund trading at a wide discount for even longer due to others' sentiment.

In general, the fund's 9.66% NAV rate is a bit of a yellow flag. This is the level that the underlying portfolio has to earn to 'cover' the distribution and not see the NAV erode. One benefit of a wide discount is that the share price distribution yield then works out to an even more attractive 10.63%.

That said, the fund doesn't only have to earn 9.66% but also the operating expenses of 1.44% on top of that. That would push us to ~11% returns being required, and to consistently do that after a very strong last few years in the market, becomes more difficult to achieve.

The fixed-income sleeve helps to generate some relative stability in terms of coverage, as interest/dividends being received can be more predictable. With the latest semi-annual report, the fund's NII coverage came to almost 39%—which was up fairly meaningfully from the ~22%. As leverage costs ease, NII will also likely creep a bit higher from here, too.

The fund's distribution has tended to be characterized by large classifications of return of capital. This would be both destructive and non-destructive, depending on the period.

When the fund initially launched, it saw losses come quite quickly based on the higher rate environment. That resulted in NAV/share price losses quite rapidly, but along with that also came capital loss carryforwards. So even in a year when the NAV rose or stayed flat in the last couple of years, we are still seeing ROC distributions.

NMAI's Portfolio

The overall asset allocation of the fund invests primarily in common stocks as the largest breakdown of the portfolio. However, at just under 50%, it means it is fairly split. It is simply that the fixed-income sleeve is broken up even more granularly, which carries corporate bonds, asset-backed securities, senior loans, preferred, as well as other various smaller allocations.

Several years ago, when we gave the fund a look, it was similarly positioned, so this would appear to be where the fund managers felt the most comfortable. With a new management team on board, we'll have to pay attention to see if this shifts at all moving forward. As we know, the investment policy has not changed, so I suspect allocations will still remain fairly similar in the years to come.

Geographically speaking, we have seen a more material shift toward U.S. exposure over a heavier emphasis on international positioning. The latest update shows that U.S. allocation is over 70%, while previously it was under 60%. Not necessarily the most dynamic shift, but it is more noteworthy than the overall breakdown of the fund.

The United States has enjoyed some strong results over the last few years in terms of equities, but international equities outperformed in 2025.

In a look at the credit quality of the fixed-income investments, it tends to be pretty split. The below-investment-grade credit, with BB and B being fairly large allocations, is balanced by a heavier allocation to 23.8%. Junk-rated credit, including not rated, comes to 52.5%, leaving around 47.5% to higher quality of BBB and above.

The split between investment grade and below investment grade means that the fund is more sensitive to interest rate changes than compared to specifically high-yield funds, but the trade-off is also lower relative risk. In an uncertain world currently, some lower risk feels appropriate.

In looking more specifically at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG) is by far the largest allocation, though not too substantially overweight in general. With that, we can see that most holdings don't carry a heavy concentration, and that highlights just how diversified this portfolio is.

Overall, the fund lists 1,470 holdings, and that really drives home just how diversified this fund is. No single holding will really impact the results of this fund on its own; instead, it will generally be a reflection of how the broader global economics are doing.

Conclusion

NMAI trades at a meaningful discount, though narrower than its historical average level; it is wider than most of its peers. That being said, the fund has also provided some mediocre results relative to peers as well—which could make its wider relative discount appropriate. With that, the fund doesn't look excessively cheap, but neither does it look overly expensive. For investors looking for significant diversification that provides a hybrid allocation between equity and fixed income, this could be an appropriate fund.

data.title: Buda Juice: A Cold Chain Story With Hot IPO Expectations
data.description:
Lately, I've been poking around newly public names, looking for the occasional diamond the market seems to be missing.

Phoenix Education (PXED) turned out not to be a diamond, but it’s a cheap little cash generator. Rich Sparkle (ANPA) ran up triple digits without much in the way of fundamentals after jumping from financial printing into TikTok sales.

But have you ever heard of Buda Juice (BUDA)?

It's a small name that hasn't seen much attention yet, even from investors who follow IPOs closely, and after all, we're talking about a company that only went public a few weeks ago.

Buda hit the public market on January 8th, even though they’d filed way back at the end of October. They put 2 million shares out there, and the CEO, Horatio Lonsdale-Hands (also one of the founders), increased the offering by selling 666,667 of his shares—roughly a quarter of the total.

So, we’re looking at a company that:

  1. Got a really small float, which makes it quite volatile;
  2. Remains under the control of its founders, Horatio and Bernard. Meanwhile, Bryan Herr, founder of Country Fresh, is another major player, owning close to 20% of the business;
  3. Around $20 million came in from the IPO, with shares set at $7.50. The stock shot up in the first days but then leveled off, now accumulating a gain close to 5%.

Shortly after going public, Buda hit a valuation north of $106 million, even with only about $12 million in revenue TTM.

Now, if you ask me why that happened, we’ll need to take a closer look at Buda’s moat—and honestly, I’ve got a few concerns about scaling it, as you would with most organic food companies.

The Company and the Moat

There isn't a whole lot to say about Buda before it got to where it is today, except that it started out in Texas as a little kiosk selling juices and soft drinks straight to customers.

By the mid-2010s, they’d shifted to a lighter, more asset-light B2C model.

Right now, they operate with a centralized production model, churning out juices and related items under their own brand (organic and non-organic) and also for private labels, mainly sold in supermarkets throughout the Southwest.

Like I said before, the part that really matters is their proprietary UltraFresh system, although it’s apparently not patented.

This is a full-cycle solution for cold-crafted juice. From harvest to pressing, everything stays at 35°F, with no thermal pasteurization, no preservatives. Shelf life sits in the 8 to 12-day range.

It’s clearly shorter than pasteurized drinks, although still ahead of most players in this space.

For a bit of color, companies like Evolution Fresh, back when it was under Starbucks (SBUX) until 2022, relied on HPP, basically using pressure rather than heat to handle microorganisms. Buda, however, says this process changes the molecular structure and makes the juice less fresh.

Other big names like Naked Juice and Odwalla always combine cold chain processing with pasteurization, since these are mass-produced drinks with a national footprint.

And right about here, a few issues I’ve noticed start to pop into my mind.

Is UltraFresh Sustainable on a Large Scale?

First off, I’m guessing you’ve noticed by now, but Buda Juice feels less like a bet on organic juice and more like a thermal logistics company that happens to handle fresh drinks.

Even though they’re currently enjoying fat margins (31.4% EBITDA compared to 13.4% for the average staple), I figure it’s mostly because they still run a tiny operation, mostly in Texas, and haven’t invested much in infrastructure yet.

Once they scale, SG&A is probably going to shoot up.

Since we know from the IPO proceeds that they’re aiming to open plants in South Carolina, Arizona, or Nevada, I’d expect the usual growing pains (negative FCF from high Capex, higher SG&A, and a hit to profitability if sales don’t pick up).

Breaking it down, about $5 million is allocated for the South Carolina plant for improvements, equipment, and operational setup to be completed later this year, while another $5 million is targeted at expanding to the West Coast next year.

That’s less than the $20 million they pulled in from the IPO, but like I said, fixed costs such as rent will eat into profits if sales don’t pick up quickly.

Another thing that worries me is that cold logistics aren’t linear. Costs and risks shoot up quickly once you try to extend routes beyond the local cluster, and big players could copy the model.

There’s little Buda Juice can do if big players copy the model.

On a small scale, it works really well, but in my opinion, the moat fades when you try to expand.

Keeping an Eye on the Numbers and Valuation

Buda Juice saw its revenue grow at a CAGR of 41.4% through FY 2024, reaching $11.2 million in sales, although it appears to have slowed down recently.

The latest numbers we have are from Q3 FY 2025, when growth dipped from double digits in the first half of the year to around 9%, leaving TTM revenue stuck at $12.2 million.

Margins remain as Buda keeps its niche Texas operations close to home. EBITDA grew 42% year-over-year to $3.7 million in FY 2024 and is holding at $3.9 million TTM, with the margin steady above 30%.

Aside from the small slowdown and the solid margins, the balance sheet looks pretty clean.

They have no debt, so it's not like they went public to cover losses, and they've generated fairly strong FCF in recent quarters.

You can see how the asset-light model churns out cash in the graph below:

Judging by these numbers, with growth for FY 2026 likely in the high single digits, the stock seems expensive (~26.6x if we consider an EBITDA of $4 million, growing 9% year-over-year), yet the bigger error would be not factoring in the two new plants.

I'm going to imagine three scenarios here to really stress-test the valuation:

  • Scenario 1 (revenue growing 20% ​​year-over-year, EBITDA margin falling 25% to 30%): revenue of $18 million and EBITDA of $4.25 to $5.4 million.
  • Scenario 2 (revenue growing 30% year-over-year, EBITDA margin remaining at 31%): revenue of $19.5 million and EBITDA of $6 million.
  • Scenario 3 (revenue growing 50% year-over-year, EBITDA margin increased by 35%): revenue of $22.5 million and EBITDA of $7.8 million.

For peers, I went with Vita Coco (COCO), Hain Celestial (HAIN), Barfresh (BRFH), Laird Superfood (LSF), and Farmer Bros (FARM). Taken together, they’re sitting at roughly 18x EBITDA on average.

Plugging in the $2.1 million in cash and roughly 12.6 million shares outstanding, I land at a price target of about $7.05 in Scenario 1, $8.75 in Scenario 2, and roughly $11.30 in Scenario 3.

And if you look at recent deals in the beverage space like Britvic, Alani Nutrition, and Vrumona, you end up closer to about 13.5x EBITDA, which would put fair value around $6.70 if you take the midpoint of the scenarios I ran earlier.

After weighing everything, I land somewhere around $7.30 per share, splitting the difference between Scenarios 1 and 2 and what recent deals suggest.

What this tells me is that a lot of the growth is already baked into the price, leaving something like 15% downside from here and suggesting the stock came out of the IPO pretty close to fair value.

It's not quite the asymmetry you're looking for in a stock with a very low float.

The upside is there, but a lot has to go right.

Sales would need to run hotter than the old CAGR; margins can't fall apart once SG&A and operating costs start creeping up.

On the flip side, it wouldn't take much for the downside to show up. A slower and more expensive expansion would be more than enough.

I'd rather sit this one out. It's a 'Sell' for me today.

Buda isn't a bad company, but the stock itself doesn't look especially attractive at these levels.

data.title: Airbnb Suffers From Premium Valuations And Maturing Growth - Mixed Return Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered Airbnb, Inc. (ABNB) in October 2025, discussing its mixed prospects entering the Q3'25 earnings season, attributed to the mixed travel indicators heading into the quarter arising from the "weaker demand growth for alternative accommodations."

Pending further growth catalysts, I had believed that the stock might continue underperforming ahead, despite the healthy balance sheet and the ongoing share retirement, resulting in my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold rating for the ABNB stock here, despite the growth initiatives already delivering expanding customer deposits, attributed to its mixed return prospects arising from the expensive valuations and the notable sideways trading cadence over the past few years.

This is pending an acceleration in its growth prospects to match the premium valuations and a further acceleration in its EPS growth from the ongoing share repurchases.

ABNB Outlines Growth Initiatives

Since my last Hold rating, ABNB has recorded an outsized recovery of +13.8% at its peak, before losing some of its recent gains attributed to the "new data that showed international tourism to the U.S. is weakening," with a similar selloff also observed in its travel peers in varying degrees.

I am of the opinion that the correction has been unwarranted indeed, since this is backwards-looking information for 2025, with readers well advised to look forward to the upcoming Q4'25 earnings season to hear about the consumer travel trends entering 2026.

If anything, ABNB has highlighted their ambition to be "the best one-stop shop for travel," with it perhaps explaining why the management has actively partnered with "both boutique and independent hotels" in numerous global cities, given the "huge supplement for our supply, especially in supply-constrained markets."

This move lends credence to my prior conjecture that the company may "expand its supply and active listings to drive growth," on top of offering "more value added services to deliver a one-stop shop travel booking experience, similar to other Online Travel Agencies" - in this case, hotels.

This is especially true since ABNB has reported a stable take rate of 13.3% on a YTD basis (-0.1 points YoY), based on the YTD revenues of $9.46B (+9.7% YoY) and the Gross Booking Value (GBV) of $70.9B (+10.4% YoY).

When comparing to its direct Online Travel Agency (OTA) peers, including:

  • Expedia (EXPE) at 12% on a YTD basis (-0.1 points YoY), and
  • Booking (BKNG) at 14.3% on a YTD basis (+0.1 points YoY), respectively,

it is apparent that ABNB's moderate ADRs of $171 in FQ3'25 (+4.6% YoY) against EXPE's at $210 (+1.9% YoY) and BKNG at approximately $153 (+5.5% YoY, author's estimates based on gross bookings and room nights), are not a cause of concern, with things likely to sequentially grow once hotels/services/experiences comprise a larger part of the former's booking value.

If anything, ABNB has hinted at further outperformance in FQ4'25, based on the guidance of "our implied take rate in Q4 2025 to be relatively flat year-over year," with "our GBV to grow low-double digits year-over-year. We anticipate our GBV to benefit from a modest increase in ADR, primarily due to price appreciation and FX, as well as continued growth in Nights and Seats Booked."

For reference, the company previously reported a take rate of 14.2% in FQ4'24, based on the revenues of $2.5B (+12% YoY) and the GBV of $17.6B (+13% YoY), with FY2025 likely to match FY2024 take rates of 13.5% (inline YoY).

Moving forward, ABNB has intensified their efforts to drive booking growth by offering longer lead time bookings through flexible payment schedules through:

  • the roll-out of Reserve Now, Pay Later offering within the US, with the zero-upfront payment already driving "the acceleration of Nights and Seats Booked in Q3, and we’ll continue rolling it out more broadly in the coming months," and
  • the fee free installments of up to 6 months in Brazil, with it "already accounted for nearly 50% of total bookings value in Q3."

This is on top of the heightened marketing efforts by:

  • "leveraging social partnerships in Japan,"
  • "tapping cultural insights in Korea,"
  • launching Airbnb Services and Experiences, with "almost half of the people who booked an experience did not have an Airbnb stay,"
  • expanding internationally to new cities/countries, and
  • "rolling out new social features so you can share memories with co-travelers, stay in touch with the people you meet on experiences, and manage everything with simple and transparent privacy controls," amongst others,

... which are already delivering higher funds receivable and amounts held on behalf of customers (customer deposit) at $7.2B (+9.5% YoY), with it providing readers with robust insights into its future top-line performance.

Therefore, while ABNB's FY2025 adj EBITDA margin guidance of 35% (-1 points YoY) somewhat mirrors the YTD performance of 37.1% (-0.8 points YoY) and implies its deteriorating profitability, I am not overly concerned indeed, aided by the slight raise from the prior guidance of 34.5% (-1.5 points YoY) offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call.

This is especially since the margin decline is attributed to the "investments in new growth and policy initiatives," as discussed above, with these efforts likely to trigger deepened customer engagement, expanded cross-selling, and growing OTA mindshare.

This is on top of the integration of AI to deliver a more personalized experience while reducing customers' friction - all of which are likely to trigger accelerated top/bottom-line growth.

ABNB's Maturing Growth Comes At Hefty Price Tag

The same quiet optimism has been observed in the consensus forward estimates, with ABNB expected to report excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +9.9%/+11.7% through FY2027, albeit notably decelerating compared to the 5Y historical top-line growth profile at a CAGR of +18.2% and the 2Y historical bottom-line growth at +18.8%.

Despite trading sideways over the past three years, it is apparent that ABNB remains rather expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x compared to the sector median of 18.14x, despite the moderation from the 5Y mean of 52.55x.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +11.7%, it goes without saying that the stock remains inflated at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.70x, compared to the sector median of 2.31x.

A similar conclusion may also be derived when comparing ABNB's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x and 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.70x against its OTA peers, including:

  • Trip.com Group Limited (TCOM) at 9.48x/1.04x (using the same methodology),
  • EXPE at 17.86x/0.83x, and
  • BKNG at 22.59x/1.25x, respectively,

... with there remaining a minimal margin of safety here, since ABNB is likely to continue trading sideways as it grows into its premium valuations - presenting its mixed return prospects in the intermediate term.

ABNB Remains A Hold

For now, ABNB continues to trade sideways between the established support levels of the $110s since mid-2023 and the resistance levels of the $140s since early 2025, with it signaling its potential for a swing trade position before the eventual breakout.

This conjecture is not overly bearish in my view, since the stock is trading at a notable premium against my base-case fair value estimates of $74, based on the LTM EPS performance of $4.11 (+44.2% sequentially) and the sector P/E median of ~18x.

There remains a minimal margin of safety compared to my base-case long-term price target of $102.60, based on the consensus FY2027 EPS estimates of $5.70, with it further lending credence to my conjecture surrounding its mixed return prospects at current levels.

While there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +38.1% to my bull-case long-term price target of $180.60, it is contingent on ABNB sustaining its premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x moving forward - a speculative conjecture attributed to the notable moderation from historical levels and the premium against its direct peers.

This is especially since the intensified growth initiatives are multiyear strategies, likely to trigger near-term noise in its bottom-line and, consequently, volatilities in its stock price performance.

Barring an acceleration in its growth prospects to match the premium valuations and a further acceleration in its EPS growth profile from the ongoing share repurchases (-3.2% over the LTM and -9.2% from FQ2'22), I maintain my conclusion in which ABNB's near-term return prospects are likely to underwhelm, resulting in my reiterated Hold (Neutral) rating here.

data.title: Platinum On Pace To $3,000 - Will It Have A Safe Path To The Milestone? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Even with EU-US tensions subsiding, the metals complex remains white-hot, tearing through fresh record highs by the session.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR), the benchmark for the sector, is now trading less than $100 away from the key $5,000 milestone.

Silver (XAGUSD:CUR), which showed some hesitation during the peak of the Greenland scare, has resumed its ascent and is on a clear path to $100 per ounce.

Despite tighter margin requirements, the supply bottleneck is dominating price movements; industrial users have no choice but to pay up for physical delivery.

However, platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) is carving out its own narrative.

While gold and silver dominate the headlines and volume, white gold is up an impressive 200% since January 1, 2025, rivaling the 245% gain seen in silver over the same period.

While supply response may eventually trigger a correction, the current squeeze is undeniable. Late participants to the rally are piling into platinum, driving it to new record levels.

For those who did not know, platinum is actually rarer than gold. It has less attraction for central banks but still represents the top-notch of precious metals.

We will now examine the technicals of XPTUSD:CUR through a multi-time frame analysis to see why it may be the favored trade in the days ahead.

Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) Multi-Time Frame Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

data.title: Kinetik Holdings: Strategic Permian Infrastructure With Strong Yield And Long-Term Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Kinetik Holdings (KNTK), I highlighted their undervaluation, while the company carries significant strategic importance, with key US oil and especially natural gas infrastructure and very significant growth potential thanks to the current investment cycle.

With a solid cash flow reported despite the massive CAPEX and even a slowdown combined with weaker commodity prices, KNTK remains in a good position to take advantage of significant long-term tailwinds, reiterating their Buy rating.

Internal Developments

KNTK’s Q3 was mixed, beating the market’s expectations on revenue but missing on EPS, as a result of slower commercial ramping at Kings Landing in August and September, capacity constraints, and higher cost of goods sold on the Delaware South system.

Meanwhile, the CAPEX continued to be elevated, spending about $154 million during the quarter and delivering $51 million in FCF, advancing the Kings Landing and ECCC (Eddy to Culberson Connector) projects of which they are 100% owners and operators, for now, and closing the 27.5% equity sale for EPIC Crude for up to $600 million, as announced previously.

Just to get an idea, they also display the significant difference between the distributable cash flow and the free cash flow (~$468.77 million vs. ~$179.16 million YTD), highlighting how the CAPEX increased over 2.6x compared to a year ago, which emphasizes the significant fundamental potential this company has.

As for the guidance, they updated it to reflect the factors mentioned above and lower commodity prices, now seeing Adjusted EBITDA of $965 million to $1.005 billion (vs. previous $1.03 billion to $1.09 billion), estimating CAPEX of $485 million to $515 million.

Financially, based on KNTK’s latest report, we continue to see a similar position, with current assets not covering their current liabilities (not uncommon for the industry) and increasing amounts of debt, although the company remains backed by giants like Blackstone (BX), which holds nearly half of the company, as mentioned previously.

As of the Q3 report, KNTK completed $176 million in buybacks over 9M’25, $100 million of which was in Q3 alone (part of their $500 million buyback program), for a YTD yield of ~2.78%. Meanwhile, in the latest Form 8937 for Q4, they anticipate the 2025 distributions to be 15% taxable dividend and 85% return of capital, while the dividend yield currently stands at about 8%, meaning roughly $505.6 million per year, which is covered by the distributable cash flow that excludes the growth CAPEX. Still, with Kings Landing becoming operational, this might be safe if they lower the CAPEX, but continued pressure from the environment can be a risk to consider.

The company’s Q4 report is expected on February 25th, with similar revenue being expected but a hit to their EPS. Since this will be the final report of the previous year, I’m looking forward to the company’s guidance and insights into the current environment, as they can provide a lot of important information for other companies that I follow, while also giving an update regarding their CAPEX expectations.

The management also mentioned the following during the Q3 Earnings Call, which highlights their accountability and business owner mentality to some degree, while also reiterating their key (~8%; ~6% for the CEO alone) ownership of the business:

Over the past 4 quarters, we have stumbled, and we recognize that we need to do better. We have had some challenges as we've integrated the Delaware North system into our business, such as the delays for King's Landing to reach service. Meanwhile, we've endured challenging and turbulent macro commodity and inflationary headwinds this year. These are not excuses. These are just facts. The buck stops with us. And as the largest individual owner of this company who has never sold 1 share, we will absolutely do better, and I will not rest until we do.

Macro Developments

To begin with the situation in Venezuela, since that can be seen as key for the oil market, I don’t see this as such a significant risk for a company that’s key for the Permian like Kinetik. As a note, this is not because they are a midstream company that’s affected less by commodity price volatility (even though 85% of their gross profit comes from fixed fees), as they can still face pressure from weaker commodity prices since that can hurt their clients and ultimately their own revenues.

Even if the US manages to replace the country’s leadership fast, it can still take years for US oil companies to actually follow. Although the White House stated that “all of our oil companies are ready and willing to make big investments in Venezuela that will rebuild their oil infrastructure,” the companies are unlikely to rush in.

At today’s levels, oil is at a point where it can pressure companies into rethinking their CAPEX plans and focus on defending their current operations instead of aggressively expanding, while companies like Exxon (XOM) even called Venezuela currently “uninvestible” in a quick way to summarize their stance, as even if they had the money for it, they likely still had better opportunities when taking into account the risk it carries.

If we assume that these oil and gas giants rush into it for some reason (e.g., significant subsidies) despite the current pressure on their margins due to falling oil prices, that’s still many years worth of building infrastructure that requires significant investments, while there’s still a high security risk.

And even if they get subsidies and promises, these companies likely think further than 3 years ahead into the future, and if we see a political swing once Trump’s term is over, there’s currently nothing that guarantees any long-term reliability.

Not only that, but the oil produced in Venezuela is among the heaviest on the planet (tougher extraction, transport, complex refining), so despite the significantly higher reserves, the cost is not as attractive as the Permian’s, for example, as it requires significant refining, plus transportation and all the logistics.

Not to mention that they likely wouldn’t want to put even more pressure on the price, which is already expected to fall in the near future, as J.P. Morgan warned that there is a risk of seeing oil fall into the $30s by late 2027 in case we see significant oversupply, while more stable scenarios still estimate WTI prices of around $53/bbl to $54/bbl in 2026 and 2027. These are not terrible, especially for Permian operators, but they can still affect the supply and temporarily weaken everyone’s budgets for investing or shareholder returns.

The IEA also projected a significant global surplus during Q1 2026, potentially putting more pressure on the price in the near term. Meanwhile, on the bright side, despite what happened in Venezuela, OPEC reaffirmed their plan to hold production steady in Q1 2026, potentially preventing the price from crashing significantly, as they did in 2025 through their production hikes.

Recently, KNTK was rated a Buy at Jefferies, citing their growth prospects being discounted after disappointing, with macro headwinds (including Waha Hub discounts) playing a significant role. As they mention, the company also has room to improve their Adj. EBITDA by about $120 million to $180 million thanks to the repricing of their older NGL contracts signed a decade ago and signaling their significant potential to grow even without high CAPEX, also highlighting them as a compelling M&A target.

Risks Reiterated

At the end of the day, even if the company is still able to produce significant amounts of money, they are paying very significant dividends and buybacks while also investing a lot, which comes with higher debt at an overall bad time given the high-rate environment we are currently in, with expensive external capital (both debt and equity).

Meanwhile, there can be more delays and pressure from the environment that can lead to worse numbers than expected, potentially even putting the buybacks or dividends at risk, which the market would definitely not enjoy.

Valuation

For KNTK’s DCF to equity valuation, taking into account the CAPEX they expect this year, let’s assume that they make $180 million in FCF in 2025 as a conservative base (basically producing zero FCF in Q4 as a result of the higher CAPEX), followed by another conservative $300 million in 2026 and growing to $750 million by the end of 2030, which is significantly below their EBITDA growth expectations and possible thanks to their developments, followed by a 7.5% CAGR until 2035 thanks to the very solid potential of natural gas in the long-term, as well as a 3.5% terminal growth rate, with a relatively conservative discount rate of 10% and 161.66 diluted shares outstanding (including both Class A and C).

This way, we get to an equity value of $9.80 billion, for an intrinsic value of $60.61 per share, well above the current $39.13, which could imply that Kinetik is a misunderstood, high-growth, key infrastructure play rather than a simple midstream utility business, which should benefit significantly following this massive CAPEX cycle that’s soon expected to slow down.

Conclusions

KNTK is reiterated as a Buy, as the company is advancing well and stands to benefit from long-term tailwinds despite the recent slowdown, with the management being self-aware and having significant skin in the game.

Given their investment cycle and potential, KNTK definitely looks like an investment that requires a long-term focus, and even if there might be volatility in the near term due to macro factors, the company remains well-positioned to capitalize on the significant growth prospects of natural gas/LNG, AI’s energy demand, etc. in the future.

Key Takeaways

  • Markets: International equity markets generated strong returns in the fourth quarter, capping a year marked by shifting leadership and unusually wide style dispersion. The core benchmark MSCI EAFE Index rose 4.9% in the quarter to finish up 31.2% for 2025, outperforming the S&P 500 Index’s gains of 2.7% for the quarter and 17.9% for the year. International value had an even stronger year, with the MSCI EAFE Value Index returning 42.2%, handily outpacing its growth counterpart by over 2,100 bps, the widest dispersion among investment styles in the 50-year history of the index.
  • Contributors: Holdings in SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics and Fujitsu. Stock selection in the information technology ((IT), industrials and materials sectors. Overweight to emerging markets, stock selection in Europe Ex U.K. and underweight to Asia Ex Japan.
  • Detractors: Holding in Rakuten Bank, CNH Industrial and Tencent. Stock selection in the financials and health care sectors, underweight to health care. Stock selection in Overweight to North America, stock selection in Japan and an underweight to Europe Ex U.K.
  • Outlook: As leadership continues to broaden and valuation dispersion remains elevated, we believe the opportunity set for international value is increasingly compelling entering 2026.

Performance Review

  • The ClearBridge International Value Fund (SBIEX) outperformed its benchmark in the fourth quarter, driven by stock selection in information technology (IT) and industrials, but partially offset by financials. Our positioning in companies aligned with long-term structural themes — such as digital AI infrastructure, the energy transition and capital markets normalization — proved especially beneficial.
  • IT was the largest contributor to relative performance. In South Korea, SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics, leading semiconductor manufacturers, benefited from higher memory prices and strong pricing and volume growth as global data center buildouts accelerated, driving meaningful earnings momentum across the memory complex. In Japan, Fujitsu, an IT services and solutions provider, advanced as corporate digitization efforts and labor shortages drove demand for efficiency-enhancing technologies.
  • Industrials also contributed, supported by long-term infrastructure and electrification themes. Siemens Energy, a German manufacturer of power generation and transmission equipment, continues to benefit from rising demand for grid upgrades and gas turbines as utilities expand electricity generation capacity to meet data center power needs. Offsetting some of these gains was CNH Industrial, a global manufacturer of agricultural and construction equipment, which detracted as elevated channel inventories and depressed farm incomes weighed on near-term results; however, we remain encouraged by management’s efforts to improve profitability through the cycle.
  • Stock selection in the financials sector weighed on relative performance. Rakuten Bank, a Japanese digital banking platform, declined as higher deposit acquisition costs weighed on near-term profitability, despite our view that these investments will ultimately support a sticky customer base and benefit from rising domestic interest rates. However, these headwinds were partially offset by Lloyds Banking Group, a U.K.-focused retail and commercial bank, which performed well as investor concerns around budget-related risks proved overdone and investor focus returned to the company’s strong earnings visibility and capital return profile.
  • From a regional perspective, relative performance was supported by strong results in Europe ex U.K., reflecting favorable stock selection amid continued investment in infrastructure and electrification. Emerging markets also proved positive, with strength in our Korean holdings offsetting profit-taking in China-related exposures, including Tencent, which we believe will continue to be a significant beneficiary of the buildout in AI applications. Meanwhile, stock selection in Japan detracted, as gains in technology-related holdings were offset by weakness in interest-rate-sensitive areas, including Daito Trust Construction, which underperformed as higher policy rate expectations and cost inflation pressured margins.
  • We established a position in Roche (RHHBY), a Switzerland-based global biopharmaceutical and diagnostics company. Positive Phase III data across multiple late-stage programs meaningfully mitigates upcoming patent expirations later this decade, while mid-single-digit revenue growth, an improving pipeline in areas such as obesity and Alzheimer’s, and a discounted valuation relative to peers support our view that the stock offers rerating potential as execution improves.
  • We exited Gerresheimer (GRRMF), a German manufacturer of specialty packaging for the pharmaceutical and cosmetics industries, after losing confidence in management’s ability to execute a turnaround following repeated profit warnings and operational missteps. We believe capital is better deployed in opportunities with clearer pathways to improvement.

Outlook

  • We enter 2026 with a more stable macro environment than this time last year. Inflation has moderated globally, giving central banks room to ease, while fiscal programs — from U.S. industrial and infrastructure spending to expanded European budgets and targeted Chinese stimulus — continue to support economic activity. With the effective U.S. tariff rate already having peaked, companies that absorbed tariff-related cost pressures in 2025 should lap those headwinds, creating modest tailwinds for growth.
  • Several themes are likely to shape markets in 2026:
  1. A less synchronized monetary backdrop: The global monetary backdrop is increasingly mixed rather than uniformly accommodative. While selective rate cuts may emerge in regions such as the U.K. and parts of Europe, other central banks — including Japan and Australia — appear more inclined toward tightening or maintaining restrictive settings, and the path for U.S. policy remains uncertain. In this environment, growth outcomes are likely to be shaped less by synchronized easing and more by regional and sector-specific dynamics, with rate-sensitive housing, utilities and infrastructure benefiting where policy flexibility exists and fiscal support and structural investment sustaining activity elsewhere.
  2. Leadership expands beyond mega cap AI: While AI remains foundational, power, logistics and efficiency improvements are becoming equally important investment themes. Companies that enable the next phase of the AI cycle — rather than those solely capturing its front-end demand — are well-positioned. Regionally, China continues to build out its own AI infrastructure. As companies there are more limited in resources, they must look to quickly monetize their investments. Companies like Tencent are finding ways to profit from these investments now but still continue to trade at large discounts to similarly positioned companies in the U.S.
  3. Emerging markets retain meaningful value: Although outside our benchmark, EM remains one of the more attractively valued areas globally, trading at a roughly 40% discount to the U.S. Disinflation offering monetary flexibility, countries like Brazil and Mexico on firmer fiscal footing and an easing dollar should support flows, bolster returns and create a more fertile ground for potential alpha generation.
  4. The U.K. looks compelling: Attractive valuations, improving inflation dynamics and falling gilt yields have created a supportive backdrop — particularly for its concentration of service-oriented industries that should benefit from AI and are spared tariff headwinds and threats of excess capacity of Chinese exports.
  5. M&A could provide an additional tailwind: Deregulation, strategic repositioning and the prospect of lower interest rates may support an uptick in M&A globally. Companies will likely act more decisively in an environment with reduced policy uncertainty.
  • With a more balanced macro backdrop, healthier geographic diversification and an expanding set of fundamental catalysts, 2026 presents a more attractive opportunity than the narrowly led markets of recent years. The companies best positioned from here are those driving meaningful internal financial and operational improvements that can support long-duration value creation.

data.title: BITX: Time To Go Against The Herd?
data.description:
When I last wrote about the Volatility Shares 2x Bitcoin Strategy ETF (BITX) for Seeking Alpha, I cautioned bulls to wait for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) to regain what, I felt, was an important technical level before making any speculative trades with leverage:

From where I sit, I would wait for confirmation that Bitcoin can hold key support before reversing higher to test the 200-day MA. The level that I'm personally watching is $103k on the weekly close.

As fate would have it, the bounce above $103k in early November was extremely short-lived. After reclaiming that level, Bitcoin quickly gave it back just a couple of days later. The ensuing move was a larger drop that briefly took BTC under $81k per coin:

Since that article, BITX has a total return of -25% and Bitcoin is struggling to stay above $90k. In this update, we'll again look at Bitcoin's technical setup as well as a few sentiment indicators to assess whether a speculative BITX position makes more sense today.

Leveraged ETF Risks

We mention it every time we cover 2x funds like BITX, but analysts are obligated to repeat it; leveraged funds are highly risky and should not be viewed the same way investors would view more traditional products like futures or spot ETFs. BITX is a leveraged fund that aims to double the returns of Bitcoin. These types of products are designed only for active traders, not long-term investors. They shouldn't be held for long periods of time, as they will typically decay from daily re-balancing.

The time to buy BITX is when Bitcoin is starting a sustained move higher. Traders should obviously completely avoid it when the market is going down. But even a choppy market that lacks clear direction is a type of market where traders should avoid BITX. My view is we're currently in a market that looks more like chop than a brewing uptrend. And if Bitcoin breaks into a clear direction, I actually think it will be to the downside.

Bitcoin Charts

Before we get too far into this, I want to make a clear point; I generally prefer viewing things through a more fundamental lens than a technical one. However, with 2x products specifically, I feel that technicals are the more appropriate way to assess opportunities. There are readers or other analysts on this platform who may come to a different view from the information in the charts. That's perfectly fine. I'm simply providing my assessment of the setups. With that, the daily:

The chart above shows 50, 100, and 200 day moving averages as well as some horizontal and diagonal trend lines to consider. The yellow arrow to the far right highlights Bitcoin breaking three supports all at the same time. First, the coin has given up the big round number of $90k. Second, it has given up the 50-day moving average less than a month after reclaiming it; which puts it below all three of those MAs listed above. And third, BTC has also given up bear flag support that started in late-November. In my view, the daily chart looks quite bad.

That said, I do think the weekly looks a bit more productive. Recall what I said in early-November about BITX and the 50-week MA:

I am not personally willing to play BITX for the bounce unless Bitcoin retakes the 50-week MA on this week's close. That level is currently $102,953.

Following the publication of that article, Bitcoin closed the week above its 50-day MA before immediately giving it back the following week. It has not sniffed that MA since. It likely goes without saying, but it is incredibly important that traders are nimble with products like BITX. Playing a breakout is fine provided there are clear parameters for exiting. A fake breakout like the one we had in November was an obvious sign to take the small loss rather than letting it turn into a big one.

The good news for bulls, is the 100-week moving average continues to serve as support. Though, admittedly, the coin is once again testing that support as of article submission.

The bad news on the weekly chart is the 20-week MA crossing below the 50 - which has happened on our current candle. More importantly, the cross is happening with price below the cross. I suspect each of these MAs will serve as resistance going forward. Furthermore, the last time BTC had its 20-week cross below its 50-week MA just a few months after an all-time high price, BTC was below its 200-week MA within 6 months. Shifting to the monthly chart:

Bitcoin has clearly broken below its uptrend that connected monthly bottoms between August 2024 and April 2025. The 20-month MA has been technical support going back to November. But the coin is now below that moving average; which is currently $90k. I suspect the most likely direction for Bitcoin over the short-to-medium term is a washout down to $73-75k. That would put the coin down to its March 2024 high and serve as a very important test of conviction for all of the speculators/investors who have acquired through the spot ETFs over the last two years.

Sentiment

Anecdotally, I'm seeing clear signs of frustration from Bitcoiners on platforms like X. While Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) continue to tear the cover off the ball, Bitcoin is essentially flat over the last 10 months and down over the last 12 months. This is almost certainly contributing to apathy towards Bitcoin on social platforms. X data shows posts containing the word "bitcoin" are at 1 year lows:

Stocktwits data shows Bitcoin sentiment at a 32, or 'bearish.' Another good gauge of sentiment is the crypto 'fear and greed' index, which is currently reading 24. While this does put digital assets in the 'extreme fear' category, this index is still far from the type of low reading that would imply true capitulation:

Historically, the lows aren't in for digital assets until the 'fear and greed' index is closer to single digits. Bad sentiment, which Bitcoin clearly has, can indeed be a good time to take the contrarian viewpoint. Bitcoin has historically been viewed as an asset for a 'retail investor' crowd that can be faded at the extremes. That said, I actually don't think this sentiment is as bad as it can get yet. While it's an important thing to consider, bad sentiment like we're seeing with Bitcoin often has to get worse before it can get better.

Closing Summary

If BTC can actually retake the 50-day MA at $90k, the next level I'd watch is the 100-day moving average. As of article submission, that level is $95k. I don't hate the idea of playing a swing trade in BITX on a break over the 50. I am not personally anticipating that, but it could certainly happen. There is also a case to be made that profit from any pause in the metals trade could find its way to Bitcoin. Gold is within striking distance of $5,000 per ounce and Silver is breathing down $100 per ounce. Each of which are big round numbers that could produce profit-taking. Those profits could find their way to Bitcoin. But I'd be very careful taking any shots on BITX until there is a clear breakout over $90k. Personally, I'd rather play BITX following a true capitulation washout under $80k.

data.title: AT&T: 3 Alternatives To The 6% Yielding Preferreds Before They Move To Junk
data.description:
On our previous coverage of AT&T (T), we suggested that investors dump the common shares for Verizon Communications (VZ). While neither stock has wowed investors with the bubble-like froth of memory chip producers, the switch did help you get 7% better returns over three and a half months.

We go over some of the news flow since then and focus on AT&T's latest acquisitions and how we see that impacting the preferred shares. We also tell you why these preferreds are now so overvalued relative to the alternatives available for higher yields.

Deleveraging Days Are Done

AT&T's big move from the 2023 lows was characterized by getting their balance sheet in the right shape. Trust in management was low (and we are being generous here) and cleaning up the mess was the priority. AT&T was still a bit slow considering how big their debt load was, but it did send the right signals as the company reduced debt by $10.3 billion in 2024, after reducing it by a net $11 billion in 2023.

Alongside that, the company delivered modest EBITDA growth. This got the debt to EBITDA ratios right on track. For AT&T the magic number has always been 2.5X and management commented on this in Q4-2024 conference call.

We also expect to achieve our target of net debt to adjusted EBITDA in the 2.5 times range in the first half of this year. This keeps us on pace to commence common stock repurchases using our initial Board approved tranche of approximately $10 billion during the second half of 2025.

Source: Q4-2024 Conference Call Transcript

Investors were finally on track for a free cash flow yield in the double digits which could work evenly split between dividends and buybacks. Then these things happened.

Acquisition of fiber assets from Lumen:

Announced on May 21, 2025, an agreement to acquire substantially all of Lumen’s Mass Markets fiber internet connectivity business for $5.75 billion in cash, subject to purchase price adjustments.

The transaction is now expected to close in early 2026, subject to certain regulatory and other customary closing conditions.

AT&T will hold the acquired fiber network assets, including certain fiber network deployment capabilities, in a new, fully owned subsidiary (“NetworkCo”).

After closing, the Company plans to sell partial ownership of NetworkCo to an equity partner that will co-invest in the ongoing business. AT&T expects to identify an equity partner and close a transaction within approximately 6-12 months of closing the Lumen transaction.

In the 12-24 months following close, the impact of the transaction is expected to be immaterial to adjusted EBITDA*, adjusted EPS* and free cash flow*, and accretive over the long-term.

Acquisition of wireless spectrum licenses from EchoStar:

Announced on August 26, 2025, the acquisition of approximately 30 MHz of nationwide 3.45 GHz mid-band spectrum and approximately 20 MHz of nationwide 600 MHz low-band spectrum for approximately $23 billion in cash, subject to certain adjustments.

The transaction is expected to close in the first half of 2026, subject to certain closing conditions, including regulatory approvals.

AT&T intends to finance the transaction with cash on hand and incremental borrowings.

Following close, AT&T expects its net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio* to increase to the 3.0x range and return to a level consistent with its leverage target in the 2.5x range within approximately three years.

The Company does not expect a material impact to adjusted EPS* and free cash flow* during the first 24 months following close, with accretion to both metrics expected in the third year.

Source: AT&T Q3-2025 Press Release (emphasis ours)

For those that might not have followed this story in the past, AT&T is notorious for making really, really bad investment decisions. DirectTV is one recent example where the sale price was a fraction of their cost and cumulative capex. While the exact numbers are difficult to extract, we estimate that AT&T lost about $25 billion on this when considering capex and distributions back to it. It is not surprising that investors have gotten lukewarm on the stock since these deals were announced. They have also garnered the attention of the rating agencies.

Fitch rates AT&T Inc. and each rated subsidiaries' Long-Term Issuer Default Ratings 'BBB+'. The RWN applies to all rated IDRs, detailed later in the release, and issue-level ratings except the Short-Term IDR and commercial paper (ratings that are affirmed at 'F2' and would likely remain unchanged if Fitch downgraded the IDR to 'BBB'.

The RWN reflects Fitch's expectations of elevated EBITDA leverage, exceeding Fitch's previous negative sensitivity of 3.2x likely at least through 2027. The transaction closing is anticipated in mid-2026, and Fitch expects to resolve the Rating Watch upon closing of the transaction.

AT&T remains strongly positioned as a U.S. telecom market leader. However, higher leverage combined with a more aggressive capital return strategy heightens the credit risk relative to the company's current rating.

Leverage Moving Higher: EBITDA leverage is slightly elevated near 3.5x at June 2025 and the planned EchoStar spectrum acquisition will further increase leverage to the mid- to high-3.0x range at least through 2027. Fitch expects the spectrum acquisition to be largely debt-financed and follows the company's May 2025 announcement it will acquire fiber assets from Lumen Technologies, Inc. (CCC+/Rating Watch Positive [RWP]) for $5.75 billion. These deals should strengthen AT&T's moat in connectivity offerings but also increases balance sheet risk over the next few years.

Source: Fitch

The Preferred Shares

AT&T has two different preferred shares listed. These are:

1) AT&T Inc. 5% DEP RP PFD A (NYSE:T.PR.A)

2) AT&T Inc. 4.7% DEP SHS PFD C (NYSE:T.PR.C).

They both yield very close to 6%.

Interestingly, these are the ones that would get pushed down in to the "junk" sphere.

While we would not argue that the first rung of junk is catastrophic, we would argue that you are definitely not getting paid for holding these at a 6.06% to 6.13% yield. We will explain why below with examples.

1) We recently wrote about Globe Life Inc. JR SUB DEB (GL.PR.D).

That is not a preferred share, but a debt security with a 6.2% current yield and 6.7% yield to maturity. We will point out that both those numbers exceed the yield from AT&T preferred shares and GL.PR.D is rated BBB+. If AT&T preferred shares are downgraded, GL.PR.D will be 3 notches higher on the rating scale and still will be yielding more.

2) Of course some might argue that they really enjoy the tax advantages of preferred shares versus debt. So the next example is for those investors. RenaissanceRe Holdings Ltd. (RNR) is an A+ rated reinsurance company. Its preferreds, RenaissanceRe Holdings Ltd. 4.20% DEP PFD G (RNR.PR.G) yield 6.5%.

These are also rated the same as GL.PR.D, at BBB+.

3) We had also written about EPR Properties CONV PFD 9% SR E (EPR.PR.E). This one is actually rated lower than AT&T, but you do get a much stronger yield. The stripped yield was around 7.62% when we got in, but that has now come down to a still respectable, 7.05%.

Verdict

AT&T common shares look for middling returns. We owned these once upon a time when they were a bit undervalued, but now we have no desire to return. The preferred shares looked expensive, even when there was no threat of a credit downgrade and now look really expensive relative to the alternatives. We would use this window to move out into better alternatives.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company Deserves To Grow (Upgrade)
data.description:
One of the more perplexing companies right now on the market happens to be The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company (SMG). The last time I wrote about the business was way back in November of 2020. Even though I found it to be a fascinating prospect, I took issue with how expensive units were. There was some optimism around the company, especially in light of the strong revenue growth that it had experienced over the couple of years leading up to that point. But that was not enough to justify a bullish assessment to me.

Since rating it a Hold back then, the market ended up pushing the stock down 59.5%. By comparison, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up an astounding 93.2%. Given this massive return disparity, you might think that the fundamental picture for the business had collapsed. But that's not exactly what transpired. Yes, revenue has fallen some. But the good news is that profitability has improved significantly. Cash flows are up, and the stock is looking cheaper now than it did back then. When you add all of this together, I believe that upgrading it to a soft Buy is absolutely appropriate.

Part of being a sophisticated investor is being open to new information as it comes in. The good news for investors is that this new information is about to come out. Before the market opens on January 28th, the management team at The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, analysts unfortunately are pessimistic when it comes to revenue. And on profitability, they view a mixed bag. I do think investors would be wise to carefully evaluate the data when management does release it. Because at the end of the day, that could justify a rather quick downgrade if things don't go well.

Taking A Fresh Look At The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company

Whenever I go an extended period of time without analyzing a company, I like to dig into its operations before getting into the fundamentals. This helps me to re familiarize myself with the business, and I believe it is useful for my readers as well. Conceptually, The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company is a rather simple business. The firm focuses on branded consumer lawn and garden products in North America, and it also provides certain nutrients, lighting, and other materials, that are used for indoor and hydroponic gardening activities. This is a rather old business, dating all the way back to 1868 in my home state of Ohio. Its origins back then involved the production and distribution of lawn fertilizers and grass seeds. But today, we have a rather sizeable business, with a market capitalization of $3.42 billion.

Perhaps the best way to understand the company is to look at its operating segments. First and foremost, we have it U.S. Consumer segment, which focuses on things such as lawn care products, gardening products, and landscape products. Examples here include lawn fertilizers, water-soluble plant foods, and the like. The Hawthorne segment, meanwhile, is focused more on lighting aimed at helping growers engage in hydroponic and indoor gardening, as well as controls like insect control products.

Over the last few years now, overall financial performance for the business has been mixed. Revenue, for instance, has been a bit disappointing, dropping from $3.55 billion in 2023 to $3.41 billion in 2025. According to management, this drop in revenue was driven by a plunge in sales associated with its Hawthorne segment. Revenue plummeted from $467.3 million to $165.8 million. This is, I believe, the primary source of the company's problems. According to management, this drop was driven by lower sales volume and lower pricing. The drop in sales volume has been the primary culprit. And a lot of this has to do with the cannabis industry. That market has become oversupplied. In essence, because of a flurry of investment activity years ago, there was a huge buildup of capacity for growing there. Eventually, this caught up with the space, pushing the demand for hydroponic systems, lights, nutrients, and other related items, all lower.

It is important to keep in mind that the drop in volume would necessarily push pricing lower also. Lower demand justifies that. It's also important to note that management has been making some strategic decisions here. They have specifically scaled back the distribution of third-party brands while focusing more on their own product lines. And on top of this, they have also made the decision to exit certain product lines entirely. Management has also been pushing to sell off this part of the company in the near future. In all likelihood, that will occur early this year.

As disappointing as this might be for those interested in the space, it is worth noting that the decline in revenue here brought with it improvements in profitability. In 2023, Hawthorne reported losses of $48.1 million. But in 2025, it achieved a $2.8 million profit. So this right sizing of the business is working in favor of investors, even if it means that The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company will no longer be a cannabis centric play. This does help to explain why the share price has fallen. But when we look at everything else, the picture looks great.

Even as revenue for the company declined, the larger operating segment, the U.S. Consumer segment, reported a slight increase in revenue from $2.84 billion to $2.99 billion. This increase, according to the company, seems to have been driven by higher sales volumes for the most part. However, from 2024 to 2025, there was a bit of weakness caused by lower pricing, with management attributing at least some of that to investments aimed at consumer activation activities. I know that sounds fancy, but it's really just discounting in order to draw in new customers. As this occurred, profits for the segment did improve, rising from $454.7 million in 2023 to $572.6 million in 2025.

The slow winding down of Hawthorne, combined with the improvements that we have seen when it comes to the U.S. Consumer segment, have had a big impact on the bottom line for the company. It went from a net loss of $380.1 million in 2023 to a net profit of $145.2 million in 2025. Adjusted net profits, meanwhile, shot up from $68.1 million to $219.6 million. Other profitability metrics large I improved, with the one exception being operating cash flow. It worsened from $531 million to $371.3 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $112.3 million to $402.2 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business popped from $446.9 million to $581.1 million.

One thing that has also helped has been a reduction in overall net debt. Back in 2023, the company had net debt of $2.58 billion. But since then, the firm has reduced overall net debt to $2.07 billion. This has helped push down the interest expense for the company from 5.02% of sales to 3.77%. At first glance, this might seem minuscule. But when applied to the revenue generated in 2025, that's an extra $42.7 million in pre-tax savings. That is absolutely something to write home about.

Ideally, this should better position the company to capitalize on market opportunities. Right now, it's estimated that the consumer do-it-yourself lawn and garden space is worth about $11 billion. Around half of that is centered around control products like weed killer, insect control products, and rodent control products. But the rest of it involves garden related offerings like soils, mulch, and plant food, as well as fertilizers, grass seeds, and spreaders that all fall under the lawn category.

There are some areas that the company is focused on when it comes to 2026 specifically. For instance, it is continuing to push for the launch of solution type products under the feeding category. It's targeting the new do-it-yourself lawn care consumers with products dedicated to the younger generations. And it is investing in certain innovation and renewed advertising activities in order to grow its grass seed business. This is a huge area of opportunity when it comes to the lawn category. But that's not all. Management is also investing in the gardens space through the launch of products dedicated things like indoor and organics, and it is also investing more heavily into the controls' category, specifically with the launch of 10 new products under its Ortho brand, and additional emphasis on its e-commerce channels.

When it comes to 2026, management expects adjusted earnings per share to come in at between $4.15 and $4.35. At the midpoint, we would be looking at adjusted net profits of $245.2 million, which would be comfortably above the $219.6 million the company reported for 2025. And with EBITDA margin expected to rise at a mid-single digit rate, we are likely to see EBITDA for 2026 of around $610.2 million, which would imply adjusted operating cash flow of about $449.1 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the historical results from the last two fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are rather low multiples if you ask me. However, relative to other similar firms, the stock is more in the fair value range.

In the chart below, I stacked it up against five similar enterprises:

  • Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT).
  • The Mosaic Company (MOS).
  • Nutrien Ltd. (NTR).
  • Corteva, Inc. (CTVA).
  • FMC Corporation (FMC).

Two of them were cheaper on a price to earnings basis. Three of them were cheaper when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, three were cheaper while another was tied with it.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Those results are forecasted to come out before the market opens on January 28th. Unfortunately, there is the expectation that revenue will fall, with analysts forecasting a decline from $416.8 million last year to $356.6 million this year. But when it comes to profitability, the picture is going to be mixed. They expect, for instance, for a loss per share of $0.97. That would actually be an improvement over the $1.21 per share that the company lost a year earlier. And it would mean an improvement in profitability from a loss of $69.5 million to $56.9 million.

Adjusted earnings per share are a different animal entirely. Analysts expect a loss per share of $1.01. That compares to the $0.89 in per share earnings lost the previous year. And it would mean adjusted net losses growing from $51 million to $59.3 million. I would venture to say that adjusted earnings will be more representative of the overall health of the company. And with revenue falling, it's not unthinkable that the cash flow metrics will come in worse year over year as well. In the chart above, you can see what these figures were last year. And I would urge investors to go over these data points carefully when the news is released.

Takeaway

While The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company might not be the cheapest candidate when it comes to companies that touch on these end markets, the firm is most certainly an attractive prospect on an absolute basis. It's foray into the cannabis market unfortunately did not pan out. But I see that as only a mild inconvenience considering what has happened from a profit and cash flow perspective over the last few years now. And given the innovations that management is focused on, I believe that additional growth is likely. Because of all of this, I believe that upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ is the right choice.
